headline: The Malay grievance, read structurally: capture, not racial competition.
context: Federal procurement, GLC appointments, and Bumiputera housing discounts were built on a poverty-eradication and restructuring agenda. World Bank 2025: 87% of Malaysian income inequality is intra-ethnic. 13MP commits to needs-based reform; PuTERA35 deepens race-based targets.

cards.big: Bumiputera policy was built on two prongs: poverty eradication and ethnic restructuring. World Bank 2025 finds the bigger leak is now intra-ethnic.
cards.sub: 87% of Malaysian income inequality is within ethnic groups; only 13% is between them. Yet the 13MP and PuTERA35 point in opposite directions.

cards.big: Parliament passed the Government Procurement Bill 2025 to penalise rent-seeking and subcontracting abuses across federal contracts.
cards.sub: An Anti-Rent-Seeking Act was signalled for end-2025. Both imply 'Ali Baba' fronting is large enough to legislate against; no official leakage figure is public.

cards.big: IDEAS counted 238 political appointments to GLC and statutory body boards under the Anwar administration up to October 2025.
cards.sub: 78% of those chairperson roles lack defined qualification criteria. Down from 301 under Najib, but the qualification gap persists across administrations.

cards.big: DOSM 2024: overall income Gini 0.390; the Chinese ethnic group has the highest internal inequality (0.412). East Malaysian Bumiputera remain the poorest subgroup.
cards.sub: The KL–Sabah income gap widened from 2.5× to 2.7×. Sabah's poverty rate is 17.7%, over triple the national average.

cards.big: For Malays: the biggest income gap is now within the community, not between Malays and others. The policy framework still chases the smaller gap.
A B40 sub-quota inside the Bumi quota. An independent appointments commissioner. Workforce-verified procurement audits. Pro-Malay reform that doesn't dismantle Article 153.
